author: @marcogallazzi9049
text: I still don't understand the difference between a battery and a capacitor. Seeing this it's even harder to get 🤦🏻‍♂️🤣

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
In brief and probably overly simplified  but  storing energy in a battery is done electrochemically, and discharging a battery is also electrochemical, as in chemicals are broken up and others are made and energy is either stored or released during these chemical changes. 

On the other hand, a capacitor simply stores charges without altering chemicals. There are several ways in which charges can be stored, but it's a capacitor if it's not changing the chemistry during charge and discharge.

replies.author: @marcogallazzi9049
replies.text: @kreynolds1123 thank you for explaining. In that case, what sort of energy storage would those mechanical devices be? There's a couple of those around and they are usually called batteries too.

replies.author: @kreynolds1123
replies.text:
@marcogallazzi9049 ways a capacitor can store energy. 

We might start with looking at two flat plates and nothing but space in between as a basic capacitor. Charges builds up on  both plates. The charges tend to repel like charges on the same plate but are balanced by their attraction to the charges on the other plate. 

Now let's insert a separator inbetween two plates. Some molecules are polar having a positive side and a negative side, so that when placed in an electric field they will orientate according to the applied electric field.  It takes and stores energy to orientate the molecules.

 Some molecules may even even be stretched like a rubber band which takes and stores energy when you stretch it.  Note, we are not breaking the molecules so no chemical change, and no battery chemistry.

Now some molecules can be dissociated in a polar solvent  forming ions. Let's look at table salt, sodium chloride. In water it disasocuates to sodium (Na+) and chlorine (Cl-)  ions.  now the ions in the dielectric, water can move around changing the concentrations of the respective ions at both plates. It takes and stores energy to move these ions to a higher concentration of either at the plates.

Then let us add effective surface area to a plate with something like  activated charcoal. the charcoal conducts electrons to one plate and leaves electron vacancies at the other plate. With many small cavities in charcoal, it can take lots more ions shielding them from other ions. Now water breaks down at 1.24v applied voltage, but doesn't conduct at voltages below that. So this type capacitor is limited to very low voltage, but given the plate  dimensions they  have a super capacitance amount of charge at that can be stored at that voltage.

There are additional ways some  capacitors might store energy, but this comment is getting long and you can do your own research. 😁

replies.author: @marcogallazzi9049
replies.text: @kreynolds1123Thank you very much for this. I will have to read it a couple of times, there´s a lot to digest :) You put some very intereristing subjetcts, i will have to research some more.

author: @Equelan2
text: There are rumours saying that salty water may turn to HCl which may burn cells inside out due to clorine ions bonds with Hydrogens. Also That stainles steel may produce a substance called Hexavalent Chromium which is reported as highly carcinogenic but i am not sure. I recommend using Epsom Salt or Alum instead of NaCl as electrolye.

author: @TheBlibo
text:
Hi 
I have a question is this working as a capacitor or a battery? To me it seems to have more in common with a simple lead acid cell as it uses an electrolyte and capacitors use non conducting dielectrics. Please correct me if I have the wrong end of the stick. Keep up the good work all very interesting 👍

replies.author: @brilliantgamestudios
replies.text: It's a capacitor.  But something as simple as changing the electrolyte to something like zinc sulfate, can make it a battery capacitor hybrid.

author: @stevecummins324
text:
Not sure if the carbon actually does much there, other than being a conductor. I say that because stainless steel forms a very thin layer of chromium oxide layer on it's surface. if that is a dialectric (metal oxides often are) then that layer will be a source of the capacitance 
have a play with with titanium sheet! Not difficult at all to anodize at a voltage of say 5V.  Once current drops to aproximately zero... the resulant capacitor has an effective breakdown voltage of 5V( but any breakdown just starts anodises it to the high voltage) and aprox 300mF per cm^2 plate area.

replies.author: @stevecummins324
replies.text: ...double the anodizing voltage, double the oxide layer thickness, and quarter the capacitance value etc

author: @HtwoHH
text: thanks for the video! what electrolyte would we think about for higher voltage? or is that information i would find in your books?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: there are lots of papers and suggestions for higher voltage electrolytes mate - water in salt being a good example also deep eutectic solutions or redox additive electrolytes - there are such a lot and we have done quite a few videos on them I would suggest a google scholar search if that's ok as you will turn up a load of interesting ones - but some of them you might find hard to resource or to make and there are lots and lots options

replies.author: @ezequieladrianminniti6252
replies.text: Yeah, also as noted, its not a good idea to go with higher voltages because it will cause water electrolysis. Unless you are using DES or water in salt electrolytes.
